God’s providence governs even the smallest concerns of people. Not a sparrow falls to the ground without him. Consider the steps that Providence took toward advancing Mordecai. The king could not sleep when Providence had a purpose, by keeping him awake. We read of no illness that disturbed his sleep, but God, whose gift sleep is, withheld it from him. He who commanded one hundred and twenty-seven provinces could not command one hour’s sleep.

See how people's pride deceives them. The deceitfulness of our own hearts appears most in the opinion we have of ourselves and our accomplishments; against which we should constantly watch and pray. Haman thought the king loved and valued no one but himself, but he was deceived. We should suspect that the esteem others profess for us is not as great as it seems to be, so that we may not think too well of ourselves nor trust too much in others. See how Haman is struck when the king commands him to do honor to Mordecai the Jew, the very man whom he hated above all men, whose ruin he was now planning!

Mordecai was not puffed up with his honors; he returned to his place and the duty of it. Honor is well bestowed on those who do not think themselves above their business. But Haman could not bear it. What harm had it done him? But that will break a proud man's heart, which will not break a humble man’s sleep. His doom was read to him from this event by his wife and his friends. They plainly confessed that the Jews, though scattered through the nations, were special objects of Divine care. Miserable comforters they are; they did not advise Haman to repent but foretold his fate as unavoidable. The wisdom of God is seen in timing the means of his church’s deliverance so as to manifest his own glory.
